entities.description: A public electronic text of Wright's complete three-page article, produced within the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse by manual keying from a public-domain edition with in-house proofreading and editing. It preserves one modern introduction, nine recipe paragraphs, fourteen notes, three printed-page milestones, and Wright's signature.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item CME00137
notes: TEXT/@LANG='enm eng' describes medieval and editorial layers together. Latin and the Chaucer quotation occur only in Wright's notes.

rights.basis: U-M labels the item Public Domain / No Copyright-United States, but the public page does not separately define the scope of that statement for markup, data, and interface elements.
rights.component: U-M electronic text and displayed encoding
notes: This records the provider's item-level statement, not an independent conclusion that every encoding contribution is uncopyrightable.

entities.description: An anonymous Middle English prose collection or manuscript-specific sequence of practical directions for pigments, metal-leaf application, iron hardening, and white-lead manufacture. Wright and the U-M XML divide it into nine procedural paragraphs; BL and METS describe eight manuscript articles because the gold-foil continuation belongs to the silver-foil article.
entities.label: Craft Recipes from Harley MS 2253, f. 52v
notes: Composition date, source exemplars, authorship, and original organization remain unknown.
This entity describes the intellectual content, not Wright's article as a bibliographic object and not Harley MS 2253 as a physical codex.

rights.basis: The sole witness is a medieval addition made centuries before modern copyright terms; this statement concerns the historical text only, not editions, transcriptions, metadata, or photographs.
rights.component: medieval recipe text

entities.description: The unique known manuscript witness of the target collection, written by the later hand conventionally called Scribe C in a column and a half of blank space on f.52v at the end of Booklet 3. bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright, 'Early English Receipts for Painting, Gilding, &c.,' Archaeological Journal 1 (1844): 64-66.
bibliographic.title: Early English Receipts for Painting, Gilding, &c.

dateStatements.label: serial publication
dateStatements.notes: Article/issue year. The completed volume's title page is dated 1845.

entities.description: The immediate printed source of the U-M encoding. Wright supplies a modern English introduction, transcribes nine Middle English recipe paragraphs from Harley MS 2253 f.52v, adds fourteen lettered explanatory and textual notes, and signs the article T. WRIGHT.
entities.label: Thomas Wright, Early English Receipts for Painting, Gilding, &c. (1844)
notes: Wright's introductory dating claim is edition history, not silently accepted as the recipe collection's composition date.
The Chaucer quotation in note i is Wright's editorial comparison and is not manuscript recipe content.

rights.basis: Wright's article was first published in 1844 and is in the public domain in the United States by age. This claim concerns the historical edition and its contents, not U-M's later electronic encoding or interface.
rights.component: Wright's 1844 article text, transcription, introduction, and notes

next_steps: Inspect Ker's introduction and Clarke's discussion of the Harley recipes.
Consult current paleographical work specifically on Scribe C and f.52v.
Do not normalize the aggregate c.1330-40 Part II date onto f.52v unless a source explicitly includes the added hand.
openQuestions.question: What component-level evidence can resolve Wright's in-or-soon-after-1307 date, the MED's ?a1350 citation, and Scribe C's relative chronology?

next_steps: Trace Scribe C hand identifications and linguistic analyses in manuscript scholarship.
Seek item-level LALME or eLALME evidence rather than borrowing the whole-codex Ludlow attribution.
Keep work composition place, witness copying place, and main-scribe activity distinct.
openQuestions.question: Who was Scribe C, and can the hand or recipe text be localized independently of the main Ludlow scribe?

next_steps: Collate f.52v, Wright, METS, and Clarke against recipe indexes and analogues.
Determine whether the gold-foil continuation has independent transmission evidence or should remain subordinate to art.15.
Avoid treating each XML P as an independently authored work without textual evidence.
openQuestions.question: What source traditions and item boundaries underlie the eight manuscript articles and nine Wright/U-M procedural paragraphs?

next_steps: Recheck the British Library digital service after recovery.
Request the physical manuscript in London if necessary.
Consult the requestable Ker facsimile while preserving its partial-codex and copyright status.
openQuestions.question: When will a current first-party image of f.52v be available for direct layout and scribal verification?

next_steps: Compare Wright line by line with direct f.52v photography or Ker's facsimile.
Consult any journal editorial correspondence or later assessments in Clarke.
Treat Wright's visible paragraphing and notes as edition features until manuscript correspondence is verified.
openQuestions.question: What explicit editorial policy governed Wright's expansions, punctuation, letterforms, word division, and treatment of manuscript gaps?

next_steps: Request a path-specific data/XML rights clarification from the repository maintainer.
Do not broaden software-worded root license text to upstream editions, U-M encoding, catalog records, scans, or manuscript images.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME root Unlicense intentionally apply to XML markup and data in CME00137?

reviewNotes: The cataloged intellectual subject is the anonymous Middle English collection or sequence on f.52v. The physical witness, Wright article, journal carrier, U-M encoding, repository XML, modern editions, scans, catalogs, and scholarship remain distinct entities.
Eight is the manuscript-article count in the current BL/METS inventory; nine is the Wright/U-M paragraph count. The graph preserves both without creating nine independently authored medieval works.
The two XML L elements are Chaucer lines quoted inside Wright's footnote i. They belong to edition paratext and do not make the medieval work verse or mixed-form.

The British Library component record explicitly excludes f.52v from the Harley scribe's work. Its c.1330-40 date, West Midlands/Ludlow context, and whole-part language profile are therefore not transferred mechanically to Scribe C or the recipes.
Wright's 1844 article date and the 1845 title-page date of the completed bound volume describe different bibliographic layers and are preserved separately.
The Internet Archive PDF is complete relative to the 482-page provider scan and full bound volume. Its target is PDF pp.82-84, scan leaves n84-n86, printed pp.64-66; it is not a crop of the article.

The METS PDF is complete relative to all three edition volumes. qpdf succeeds with linearization hint-table warnings; pages render and text extracts, so the warning category is disclosed rather than treated as missing content.
The Sobecki cache file is the complete provider-published 50-page open introduction but partial relative to the 2020 book. It is never described as a complete book download.

Public access is not a reuse license. Rights are separated for the medieval text, Wright edition, U-M encoding, repository XML, IA derivatives, BL catalog metadata, manuscript images, Ker facsimile, METS edition, Sobecki chapter, and Clarke edition.

sourceLinks.entityLabel: Thomas Wright, Early English Receipts for Painting, Gilding, &c. (1844)

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan item CME00137, a manually keyed and proofed electronic text of Thomas Wright's 1844 article 'Early English Receipts for Painting, Gilding, &c.' Wright transcribed the added Middle English craft directions on British Library Harley MS 2253, f.52v, supplied an introduction and fourteen lettered notes, and printed the article on pp.64-66 of Archaeological Journal volume 1. The serial issue is dated 1844, while the bound-volume title page is dated 1845. The XML contains nine recipe paragraphs; current British Library and METS descriptions number eight manuscript articles because Wright's separate gold-foil paragraph is a continuation within METS art.15. The f.52v addition is excluded from the main Harley scribe's c.1330-40 copying: current scholarship assigns it to the later hand called Scribe C, probably not much later than the main hand. Wright's in-or-soon-after-1307 estimate and the MED's ?a1350 citation remain unresolved competing claims. A complete 482-page Internet Archive scan and complete OCR/scandata/metadata derivatives are cached, as is the complete 1,448-page METS three-volume edition. British Library catalogs full manuscript image coverage but currently marks the images unavailable; the 1965 Ker facsimile and 2016 Clarke edition remain findable through library access routes.

catalogingSubject.label: Craft Recipes from Harley MS 2253, f. 52v
catalogingSubject.scope_note: One anonymous, manuscript-specific collection or selection of Middle English craft directions copied by the later hand conventionally called Scribe C into blank space on British Library Harley MS 2253, f. 52v. CME00137 represents nine prose recipe paragraphs in Thomas Wright's 1844 transcription: the current British Library record and the complete METS edition instead number eight manuscript articles because the gold-foil direction continues the silver-foil article. Wright's modern introduction, fourteen footnotes, two quoted Chaucer lines, and signature are edition paratext, not medieval work content.

contentParts.label: Wright's editorial frame and apparatus

locators.value: DIV1/HEAD; DIV2[@TYPE='introduction']; fourteen NOTE elements in DIV2[@TYPE='recipes']; CLOSER/SIGNED

notes: The two Chaucer lines in footnote i belong exclusively to this editorial frame.
contentParts.summary: Modern heading, article title, one-paragraph introduction, fourteen lettered footnotes, and signed closer surrounding the medieval transcription.

contentParts.label: Vorte make cynople
contentParts.summary: Directions for making cynople by boiling brazilwood in ditch water and pouring the reduced liquid into a cavity in chalk to cool under cover.

contentParts.label: Vorte temprene asure
contentParts.summary: Directions for tempering fine azure with gum water, suppressing bubbles with earwax, or repeatedly purifying inferior azure with prepared egg white before adding gum water.

contentParts.label: Vorte make grasgrene
contentParts.summary: Directions for making grass-green by grinding verdigris and mixing it with the finest plant juice available.

contentParts.label: Vorte maken another maner grene
contentParts.summary: Directions for another green, tempering verdigris with juice from a rotten apple.

contentParts.label: Yet for gaudegrene
contentParts.summary: Directions for gaudy-green, tempering verdigris with pennywort or gladden.

contentParts.label: Vorte couche selverfoyle
contentParts.summary: Directions for preparing a chalk, gum, and egg-white size, laying silver foil, and burnishing it with a dog's tooth.

contentParts.label: Ithe selve maner mac the sise to goldfoyl
notes: Counted as the seventh Wright/U-M procedural paragraph but not as a separate manuscript article in the METS inventory.
contentParts.summary: A gold-foil variant of the preceding size, adding red ochre to strengthen its color beneath the gold.

contentParts.label: Vorte maken iren as hart as stel
notes: The one LG/two L in this XML paragraph occurs inside Wright's note i, not inside the medieval iron-hardening prose.
contentParts.summary: Directions for wrapping an iron tool edge in powdered argul, heating it red-hot, and quenching it in water.

contentParts.label: Vorte maken blankplum
contentParts.summary: Directions for purifying and suspending thin lead strips over vinegar in a sealed vessel buried in horse dung, then collecting the resulting white lead.

notes: Wright ties the text to the beginning of Edward II's reign. The claim conflicts with the current MED source date and with modern recognition that f. 52v is a later addition by Scribe C.

dateStatements.display: A later addition by Scribe C, probably not much later than the c.1330-40 main hand

scope.description: Relative copying chronology for the f. 52v addition

notes: The serial article is dated 1844; the completed bound volume's title page bears 1845. The two carrier dates remain separate in lineage.

scope.description: Publication of Wright's transcription in Archaeological Journal volume 1

extent.description: Nine Middle English prose recipe paragraphs in Wright and the CME XML, all on manuscript f.52v and all represented below as content items. The current BL record and METS catalog eight manuscript articles (arts.10-17) because the gold-foil procedure is a continuation within art.15, not a separately numbered article. The encoded article has 10 P elements total (one Wright introduction plus nine recipe paragraphs), 14 NOTE elements, 3 PB elements, 2 DIV2 elements, and one LG with 2 L elements inside footnote i. Those two quoted Chaucer lines are modern editorial paratext and are excluded from intrinsic-work metrics.

formStatements.description: Nine Middle English procedural paragraphs in the Wright/U-M representation, corresponding to eight manuscript articles in the METS inventory. The XML's one LG and two L elements quote Chaucer inside Wright's footnote i and are excluded from the medieval collection's form.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English in Wright's introduction and notes, Latin quotations in those notes, and the two Middle English Chaucer lines in note i are edition paratext rather than additional language facets of the cataloged recipe collection.
The preferred metadata title is a supplied collection title. Wright's article title and the British Library's aggregate component label remain alternates at their own descriptive layers.

The nine-item count is reproducible for Wright's printed paragraphs and the XML P structure; the eight-article count is reproducible for the manuscript inventory. Neither count alone proves authorial organization.

Wright's fourteen footnotes include Modern English and Latin material. Footnote i also quotes two Middle English Chaucer lines; all are edition paratext and do not change the work's Middle English prose classification.

No work-level place or dialect statement is asserted. British Library Ludlow and West Midlands data describe the codex and main Harley scribe, while the same record explicitly excludes f.52v from that hand.

Rights and access for the medieval text, Wright edition, U-M encoding, repository XML, provider scans, modern editions, catalog metadata, and manuscript photography are recorded separately in lineage.

next_steps: Consult Ker's 1965 introduction, Clarke's 2016 edition, and current paleographical scholarship for an explicit component-level date.
Preserve Wright's in-or-soon-after-1307 estimate, the MED's ?a1350 citation, and the relative Scribe C chronology as distinct claims.
Do not substitute the c.1330-40 date of the surrounding main-scribe component for f.52v.
openQuestions.question: When were the recipes composed and when exactly did Scribe C copy them onto f.52v?

next_steps: Consult Scribe C hand studies, the Ker facsimile introduction, LALME-related work, and current manuscript scholarship.
Keep the whole-codex Ludlow and West Midlands context out of work-level place and dialect facets until the added hand is independently localized.
openQuestions.question: Can Scribe C be identified or localized independently of the main Ludlow scribe?

next_steps: Collate the witness and modern editions against recipe indexes and analogues.
Treat the gold-foil paragraph as a procedural unit within METS art.15 unless manuscript layout or source scholarship establishes a distinct article.
Do not create nine independently authored works from encoding paragraph boundaries alone.
openQuestions.question: Do the eight manuscript articles or nine Wright/U-M paragraphs derive from one earlier recipe collection, multiple exemplars, or separately circulating procedures?

next_steps: Recheck the British Library digital image service after recovery.
Request Harley MS 2253 in London or consult Ker's 1965 facsimile under its library access conditions.
Compare direct images with Wright, METS, and Clarke before asserting diplomatic features not visible in the current public sources.
openQuestions.question: Can f.52v be inspected in a current first-party digital image or in person to verify layout, exact boundaries, and scribal features?

next_steps: Seek a path-specific XML or data rights statement from the repository maintainer.
Do not broaden the repository root wording to Wright's edition, U-M's encoding, the medieval text, or manuscript images.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME repository's software-worded root Unlicense intentionally cover the XML markup and data in CME00137?

notes: No named author, translator, or compiler is established. Scribe C is the copyist of the witness addition and is not converted into an author or compiler without evidence.

scope.description: Selection or gathering of the craft directions as transmitted together on f. 52v
Wright's 1844 article, explanatory introduction, transcription, and fourteen footnotes
The Middle English recipe text printed from Harley MS 2253, f. 52v

subjects.label: Applying and burnishing silver and gold leaf

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose collection or sequence of practical craft directions preserved on Harley MS 2253, f. 52v. The procedures explain how to prepare cynople, temper azure, make three green pigments, size and burnish silver and gold foil, harden an iron edge, and manufacture white lead. The sole known witness is a later addition by the hand called Scribe C in blank space at the end of Booklet 3.

The nine Wright/U-M paragraphs cover: cynople from brazilwood; azure tempered or purified with gum water or egg white; grass-green with verdigris; another green with rotten-apple juice; gaudy-green with pennywort or gladden; silver-foil sizing and burnishing; a gold-foil variant of the same size; iron hardening with argul; and white-lead manufacture in a sealed vessel above vinegar and horse dung.

CME00137 encodes Wright's complete three-page article on Archaeological Journal printed pp.64-66: one modern English introduction paragraph, nine Middle English recipe paragraphs, fourteen lettered footnotes, three page-break markers, and Wright's signature. Its single two-line verse group is a Chaucer quotation inside footnote i and is excluded from work form and verse extent.

Nine is an edition/encoding paragraph count, while eight is the current manuscript-article count: Wright and U-M place the gold-foil continuation in its own paragraph, but METS keeps it within article 15, 'Vorte couche selverfoyl.' The preferred title and collection boundary therefore support discovery without asserting nine independently authored works or nine authorial manuscript divisions.

notes: A supplied discovery title is necessary because the manuscript and current catalogs provide item incipits or aggregate descriptions rather than an authorial collection title.
